macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: Admon and Chanan, Two Judges Who Made Decrees

micro_segments.title: Opening and Context of the Final Perek of Ketubot
Identification of Admon and Chanan ben Avishalom as the Two Judges
Case One: Wife Demanding Support When Husband Goes Overseas
Chanan's Position: Oath Only at the End
Sons of High Priests Dispute: Oath at Beginning and End
Rabbi Yochanan ben Zakkai Agrees with Chanan
Tosafot Discussion on the Name Avishalom

macro_segments.title: Gemara Discussion: Two versus Three Judges and the Terms Gezeirot versus Gezeilot

micro_segments.title: The Braita Presenting Three Judges of Theft (Gezeilot) Rather Than Decrees
Resolution: The Mishna Lists the Most Important Judges Only
Reconciling Gezeirot and Gezeilot: They Made Rulings on Theft Cases
Braita of Rabbi Yosei on Edicts Regarding Animal Damage to Crops
Nature of Gezeirah: Judges Setting Parameters and Guidelines Rather Than Just Applying Law
Second Braita: Three Judges Including Nachum HaMadai
Nachum HaMadai's Status Among Judges of Edicts but Not Accepted by Sages

macro_segments.title: Enumeration of Judges and Courts in Jerusalem: 394 of Each Institution

micro_segments.title: Claim of 394 Major Courts, Synagogues, and Study Halls in Jerusalem
Rashi's Clarification on the Nature of These Institutions
Gemara's Clarification: We Are Speaking Specifically of Judges of Edicts

macro_segments.title: Compensation of Judges of Edicts from the Temple Treasury (Trumat HaLishka)

micro_segments.title: Judges of Edicts Received 99 Talents of Silver from the Temple Treasury
Problem: Can Judges Accept Payment Without Violating the Prohibition on Taking Fees?
Response: Increasing Compensation If Judges Refuse, to Ensure Their Sustenance

macro_segments.title: The Case of Karna: Accepting Equal Court Fees from Both Litigants

micro_segments.title: Karna as a First-Generation Amora Who Accepted Fees from Both Sides Equally
Question: How Can Karna Take Fees If the Torah Forbids Bribes?
Analysis: Bribes Corrupt When Taken from One Side Only
Gemara's Challenge: Even Equal Fees from Both Sides Should Be Forbidden

macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Fees for Judgment and Compensation for Lost Work (Sachar Batala)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: One Who Takes a Fee for Judging Voids His Judgments
Gemara's Response: Sachar Batala (Compensation for Lost Opportunity) Is Permitted
Braita: Disgusting for a Judge to Take Fees, Yet His Judgment Still Stands
Resolution: Sachar Batala Is Only Permitted When It Is Obvious the Judge Is Losing Money
Example of Karna: His Wine-Tasting Work Made His Opportunity Cost Obvious
Story of Rav Huna: Requesting Replacement for His Daily Water-Drawing Work

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Two Approaches to Compensating Full-Time Judges from Public Funds

micro_segments.title: Rabbeinu Tam's View: Payment from Public Funds Is Permissible Because Not from Litigants
Alternative View: Judges Are Supported by the Community as a Responsibility, Not Paid for Services

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Abahu on the Blinding Effect of Bribes: Even Righteous Judges Become Blind

micro_segments.title: Metaphor: A Bribe Blinds the Eyes Like a Medical Treatment Cannot Heal a Diseased Eye

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Abahu on the Blinding Effect of Bribes: Even Righteous Judges Become Blind (continued)

micro_segments.title: Verse: Bribes Blind the Open-Eyed (Exodus 23:8)
Braita: Bribes Blind Even the Wise; How Much More So the Foolish
Braita: Bribes Pervert the Words of the Righteous; How Much More So the Wicked
Gemara: Questioning Why Righteous and Wise Judges Would Be Appointed If They Were Corruptible
Resolution: Even the Righteous Become Blinded or Demented If They Accept Bribes

macro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's Teaching on King and Judge: Self-Sufficiency Establishes the Land

micro_segments.title: Verse Interpretation: A King Establishes the Land Through Justice When Self-Sufficient
Contrast: A Needy Judge Like a Kohen Seeking Terumah Destroys the Land
Rava's Addition: A Judge Who Borrows Objects Is Invalid to Render Judgment
Exception: Borrowing Is Permitted If the Judge Also Lends to Create Reciprocity
Story of Rava and Bar Maryon: Rava Borrowed Yet Remained Willing to Judge

macro_segments.title: Rava's Teaching on Bribery: The Psychological Mechanism of Unconscious Bias

micro_segments.title: Once a Judge Accepts a Gift, They Subconsciously Identify with the Giver as One of Themselves
No One Can See Fault in Themselves, So a Judge Cannot Be Objective About a Connected Person
Etymology of Shochad (Bribe): Becoming One (Chad) with the Briber

macro_segments.title: Pappa's Ruling: A Judge Must Recuse from Those Loved or Hated

micro_segments.title: A Judge Should Not Judge One Whom He Loves or Hates
One Whom He Loves: Cannot See Faults; One Whom He Hates: Cannot See Merits

macro_segments.title: Abaye and Rava on Community Perception of Rabbis: Teaching vs. Pandering

micro_segments.title: Abaye on a Torah Sage Beloved by His Community: Likely He Is Not Rebuking Them
Rava's Self-Reflection: Initially Thought All Mechuza Loved Him; Later Realized Some Hated Him
Rava's Insight: Judge Must Accept That Some Will Love and Some Will Hate Based on Rulings

macro_segments.title: Braita on Non-Monetary Bribes (Shochad Devarim): Favors and Actions as Corruption

micro_segments.title: Even Verbal Favors and Services Count as Bribes, Not Only Money
Story of Shmuel: Rejected as Judge When Man Helped Him Cross a Bridge, Mentioning Upcoming Case
Story of Ameimar: Rejected as Judge When Man Removed Feather from His Head, Mentioning Case
Story of Mar Ukva: Rejected as Judge When Man Covered His Spit, Mentioning Case

macro_segments.title: Story of Rabbi Yishmael ben Rabbi Yossi: The Sharecropper's Fruit and Unconscious Bias

micro_segments.title: Sharecropper Brings Rabbi Yishmael Fruit a Day Early, Mentioning Upcoming Court Case
Rabbi Yishmael Recuses Himself and Appoints Two Other Rabbis as Judges
While Observing the Case, Rabbi Yishmael Finds Himself Thinking of Arguments Favoring the Sharecropper
Rabbi Yishmael's Reflection: Even Without Accepting the Favor, He Is Biased Subconsciously

macro_segments.title: Story of Rabbi Yishmael ben Rabbi Elisha: First Shearings of Sheep as Bribe

micro_segments.title: Man Brings Rabbi Yishmael First Shearings from a Distance, Mentioning Upcoming Court Case
Rabbi Yishmael Recuses Himself and Again Appoints Two Other Rabbis
While Observing, Rabbi Yishmael Again Finds Himself Strategizing Arguments for the Sharecropper
Rabbi Yishmael's Reflection: Even the Benefit He Was Entitled to Corrupts His Judgment
